followup_notes.changes_from_prior_cycle: Changed recommended verdict from 'routine' to 'contested', lowered technical_soundness from 6 to 5, raised reviewer_confidence from 4 to 5. The codex reviewer apparently reviewed the PDF and provides Section III details (Table I, Figure 1, Figure 3) not visible in my HTML bundle. Their sigma convention inconsistency objection is the most substantive new concern and, if valid, would be a major statistical flaw affecting the headline Bayes factors. I have adopted this as a major objection with confidence 0.7 (lower than codex's 0.9 because I cannot independently verify it). I also adopted the scope-overstatement and source-parameter-uncertainty concerns as moderate objections. Gemini's fatal verdict and 1/10 score are not adopted—they reflect penalization of the HTML rendering artifact, not paper quality.
invalid_points_from_other_reviewers: Gemini's 'fatal' severity verdict and 1/10 overall score: these are based on the HTML rendering failure in the review bundle, not on flaws in the paper. The paper exists on arXiv; the codex reviewer accessed the PDF and provides substantive Section III content. Penalizing the paper for a LaTeXML artifact is not appropriate.

Gemini's recommended verdict of 'major_flaw': the manuscript is not missing its results; the review bundle is missing them. The actual paper appears complete per the codex reviewer's Section III references.
valid_points_from_other_reviewers: Codex (confidence 0.9): sigma convention inconsistency in the unmodelled hierarchy—if sigma=0 is the true null but sigma=1 is used in Bayes factor computation (or vice versa), the headline results are invalid. This is a major concern requiring human spotcheck.

Codex (confidence 0.85): The negative result is overstated as disfavoring 'any mass scale' when the statistic only tests variance differences in cross-correlated power with a constant-time template. Deviations in mean, sign, frequency structure, or with poor template overlap are not excluded.

Codex (confidence 0.8): Mass-threshold classification uses delta function at MLE, ignoring source-parameter uncertainty. Events with broad mass credible intervals near thresholds could shift between populations and affect sparse-bin Bayes factors.
Codex (confidence 0.65): Gaussian calibration of the alpha statistic is assumed, not demonstrated for real GWTC-3 residuals. No off-source or time-slide validation is presented.
Gemini (confidence 1.0): Notation error in Eq. (2)—dummy integration variable collides with limit variable. Minor but real.
Gemini (confidence 0.9): Possible typo in Eq. (4) denominator—K_D(t) is already an expectation value per Eq. (3), so <K_D(t)K_D(t)> may be a notation error for Var[D(t)].

main_claims.claim: SCoRe applied to GWTC-3 finds Bayes factors of 0.16–0.5 disfavoring a mass-scale threshold in the range 2.5–60 solar masses.
main_claims.notes: The mass range endpoints were stripped in the HTML bundle but codex's review confirms ~2.5–60 solar masses. The Bayes factor range is stated but depends on whether the sigma convention in the unmodelled hierarchy is internally consistent—this is disputed by codex and cannot be verified from the bundle alone.

main_claims.claim: The full-population distribution of excess cross-correlated power is consistent with noise (Bayes factor ~0.07).
main_claims.notes: Codex identifies a potential inconsistency: sigma=0 vs sigma=1 as the no-deviation model. If sigma=1 is used as the noise baseline in a likelihood that already adds +1 for measurement noise variance, the Bayes factor is computed against the wrong null. Requires human spotcheck.

main_claims.claim: The EFT power-law deviation search cannot constrain the power-law index given current detector sensitivity.
main_claims.notes: Codex confirms the section exists but is under-documented—posteriors are claimed to be uninformative up to >10^5 in effective tidal deformability, but no event-level likelihoods, sensitivity curves, or prior-dependence checks are shown.

main_claims.claim: SCoRe provides optimal (Wiener filter) inference for unmodeled deviations with correct selection-effect accounting.
main_claims.notes: The theoretical framework is internally consistent as presented. The optimality claim is referenced to prior work. The real-data calibration of the Gaussian noise assumption is not demonstrated.

main_claims.claim: This is the first application of SCoRe to real gravitational-wave data.
main_claims.notes: Plausible given the cited prior SCoRe papers used simulated data. Not independently verified.

missing_assumptions.assumption: In the unmodelled hierarchy, the no-deviation null is sigma=0 (not sigma=1), and the +1 in the likelihood variance accounts for measurement noise separately from population scatter.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Section III.A, hierarchical likelihood for alpha
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: If the paper's intended generative model uses sigma=0 as the null but the reported Bayes factors are evaluated at sigma=1 (or vice versa), the headline results are computed against the wrong hypothesis and the conclusions are invalid.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The 30 highest-SNR events are a representative sample for population-level deviation constraints; SNR-based selection does not introduce Malmquist-type bias correlated with mass.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: SNR selection suppresses low-mass events (as the paper itself notes per codex), which can distort the mass-threshold test by undersampling one population.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Residual power in the CRISP is not contaminated by correlated environmental noise (e.g., Schumann resonances) or glitches that are coherent between detectors.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The CRISP interprets coherent residual power as possible source-dependent deviation; correlated instrumental artifacts could mimic or mask this.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The standard PE posteriors used as priors in Eq. (5)–(8) do not absorb the deviation signal into waveform parameters (e.g., effective spin absorbing beyond-GR effects).
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: If PE absorbs deviation signal, CRISP will underestimate deviations and Bayes factors will be biased toward the null.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The constant-in-time power template used in Section III.A has adequate overlap with physically relevant unmodeled deviations.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Section III.A, choice of Z(t;theta) constant
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Section II.3 explicitly warns that the statistic can miss deviations with poor template overlap; the negative result is conditional on this sensitivity model.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: In the authors' intended generative model for Section III.A, does sigma=0 or sigma=1 represent the no-deviation (null) hypothesis? Is the +1 in the event likelihood variance purely measurement noise, and is sigma exclusively population scatter?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: Resolving the sigma convention requires reading the full likelihood derivation and figure captions in the PDF, then checking the actual Bayes factor computation. The HTML bundle is truncated and I cannot independently verify codex's reading of the formula.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: What are the actual numerical mass-scale endpoints in the abstract (rendered as 'M_sun and M_sun' in the HTML)?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: Numerical values were stripped by the HTML renderer; requires reading the PDF.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does Section III include sensitivity estimates (upper bounds, sensitivity curves, or injection studies) for the EFT power-law search, beyond the qualitative statement that the index is unconstrained?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: Section III.B is absent from the HTML bundle; codex confirms the section exists but characterizes it as under-documented.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does the paper include off-source, time-slide, or injection validation demonstrating that the computed alpha values for real GWTC-3 residuals follow the assumed unit-Gaussian distribution?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This would appear in Section III which is missing from the HTML bundle. Codex does not confirm its presence.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is the delta-function approximation pi(theta)=delta(theta_MLE) for source parameters in the threshold analysis quantitatively justified, and does the paper assess sensitivity to this choice for events near the threshold?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: Requires reading the full Section III.A with Table I and the surrounding discussion; not accessible from the HTML bundle.

novelty_assessment: Modest. The SCoRe framework (CRISP statistic, Bayesian population inference, Wiener filter unmodeled search) was established in two prior papers by the same authors. The contribution here is the first real-data application to GWTC-3 and the specific mass-threshold population test. The mass-threshold hypothesis is well-motivated by EFT and exotic compact object literature. The null results are scientifically informative at face value but not surprising given current detector sensitivity. The paper's value is in validating and deploying the pipeline on real data—useful but incremental relative to the prior framework papers. The EFT search adds little given it is explicitly too underconstrained to provide inference.
score_rationale: Technical soundness lowered to 5 (from 6 in cycle 1) due to the codex reviewer's substantive sigma inconsistency objection: if the no-deviation null is evaluated at the wrong sigma value, the headline Bayes factors are incorrect. Additional moderate concerns about scope overstatement, source-parameter uncertainty at thresholds, and lack of Gaussian calibration validation compound this. Novelty remains 4: this is an application paper; all key methodological ideas are from prior works. Significance is 5: first real-data SCoRe run is valuable, null results are informative, but the EFT search is explicitly too weak to constrain. Clarity is 4: the framework sections are reasonably clear, but the HTML bundle is badly damaged, the scope of the negative result is overstated in the abstract, and the EFT section is under-documented per codex. Reviewer confidence rises to 5 from 4 because the codex reviewer provides Section III context I lacked, giving a more complete picture—but I still cannot independently verify the sigma inconsistency or the full results. Overall 4.7 reflects a competent application paper with potentially significant statistical inconsistency in the main result, modest novelty, and a negative result whose scope is overstated. Verdict is 'contested' pending resolution of the sigma convention question.
summary: This paper applies the SCoRe/CRISP framework to 30 high-SNR GWTC-3 events to search for mass-scale-dependent deviations from waveform models. The core result is a negative search: Bayes factors of 0.16–0.5 disfavor a mass-threshold hypothesis, and the full-population cross-correlated power is consistent with noise (Bayes factor ~0.07 per codex). The EFT power-law search is too underconstrained to infer the index. In cycle 2, the codex reviewer—who evidently reviewed the PDF rather than the truncated HTML bundle—raises a substantive new technical concern about the unmodelled hierarchy: whether sigma=0 or sigma=1 represents the no-deviation hypothesis appears inconsistent between the generative model and the text/figures. If this inconsistency is real, it would directly affect the headline Bayes factors. I incorporate this as a major concern and revise toward a contested verdict.

technical_objections.evidence: Codex reviewer (confidence 0.9) identifies this inconsistency with reference to specific figure captions and the likelihood formula. The two conventions are mathematically incompatible and would yield different Bayes factors against the null.
technical_objections.objection: The unmodelled hierarchy appears internally inconsistent about what sigma represents. The event likelihood written with variance sigma^2+1 implies sigma=0 is the no-deviation hypothesis, but the text and figure captions repeatedly identify sigma=1 as the noise/no-deviation model. If sigma denotes observed alpha distribution width, the likelihood should not also add +1 for measurement noise.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section III.A, population prior for alpha, event likelihood after marginalization, Figure 1 caption, Figure 3 caption

technical_objections.evidence: Bundle text terminates with 'BETA / Experimental support, please view the build logs for errors' immediately after the Section III heading. However, this is a rendering artifact of the review bundle, not a deficiency in the paper itself—the codex reviewer evidently accessed the PDF and provides Table I, Figure 1, Figure 3 details.
technical_objections.objection: Section III (main results) is almost entirely absent from the HTML bundle due to a LaTeXML rendering failure. This severely limits independent auditability of the analysis from the provided source.

technical_objections.evidence: Codex reviewer (confidence 0.85) notes the statistic cannot exclude mass-dependent deviations in mean, sign, phase/time structure, frequency structure, or effects producing equal variances in the chosen statistic.
technical_objections.objection: The negative result is overstated in scope. The statistic tested is cross-correlated residual power with a constant-in-time template and a zero-mean Gaussian population model comparing variances across mass thresholds. Section II.3 itself notes that alpha can be zero for deviations that do not overlap the template. The abstract and conclusion phrase this as disfavoring 'any mass scale', which is broader than what the statistic can exclude.
technical_objections.paper_location: Abstract, Section III.A conclusion, Section II.3

technical_objections.evidence: Codex reviewer (confidence 0.8) notes this approximation with reference to Table I showing broad mass credible intervals.
technical_objections.objection: Mass-threshold event classification uses a delta function at the MLE for source parameters, ignoring parameter estimation uncertainty. Many component masses have credible intervals that straddle plausible thresholds, which could materially affect Bayes factors for sparse threshold bins.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section III.A, pi(theta)=delta(theta_MLE) approximation, Table I

technical_objections.evidence: Codex reviewer (confidence 0.65) raises this; consistent with my cycle-1 concern about non-stationary noise not addressed in the visible text.
technical_objections.objection: The Gaussian/independent-noise calibration of the alpha statistic is assumed but not demonstrated for real GWTC-3 residuals. No off-source, time-slide, injection, or posterior predictive calibration is presented to validate that real detector residuals follow the assumed distribution.
technical_objections.paper_location: Section II.1 likelihood assumptions, Section III.A

technical_objections.evidence: Paper states the assumption explicitly but provides no robustness check. This was flagged in cycle 1 and remains unaddressed.
technical_objections.objection: The Gaussian likelihood form in Eq. (4) requires CRISP bins to be uncorrelated (i≠j). Real detector noise (lines, non-stationarities, glitches) may introduce inter-bin correlations not addressed in the visible text.

technical_objections.evidence: Codex reviewer confirms this characterization. A null result without a sensitivity estimate provides little scientific constraint.
technical_objections.objection: The EFT power-law search (Section III.B) reports posteriors uninformative up to >10^5 in effective tidal deformability but provides no event-level likelihoods, sensitivity curves, or prior-dependence diagnostics.

technical_objections.evidence: Gemini reviewer (confidence 1.0) flags this; it is a standard notation error that does not affect the mathematics.
technical_objections.objection: Minor notation: the dummy variable of integration in Eq. (2) is the same symbol 't' used in the integration limits (t ± τ/2), creating ambiguity.
